- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Mon Jun 15 09:33:22 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch 15 — clear the remaining tail (12 issues, all reviewed)
    
    Closes the final pass-4 items; each fix adversarially reviewed by an
    independent agent (wf_4bb5c83b-944) — all 12 confirmed resolved, no regressions.
    
    reasoning_client:
    - #272 bound the timeout ThreadPoolExecutor: track in-flight workers, fail fast
      with a distinct "saturated" ReasoningError when slots are leaked to hung
      backends instead of silently turning all traffic into timeouts; T2 outer
      timeout gets margin over the inner HTTP timeout so the inner one frees the worker
    - #274 permission_mode="dontAsk" on the T3 SDK subprocess (never wait on a prompt)
    - #280 fold cache_read/creation tokens into T3 span token totals
    - #275 bump temperature per complete_json retry so regeneration is meaningful
      under deterministic T2 decoding; #270 retry-exhaustion give-up test
    
    langgraph:
    - #276 run checkpointer setup() once per process per conn_str (advisory lock
      still guards the first-time cross-process race)
    - #267 idempotent Gate-2 prereq-failure (no duplicate audit events on re-entry
      of the non-terminal deferred path)
    
    phoenix/observability:
    - #277 bound the shutdown flush at the OTLP exporter level (force_flush ignores
      its timeout_millis); #278 warn on global-provider collision (else T3 spans
      vanish); #281 SIGTERM flush handler (atexit misses SIGTERM); #283 loopback
      guard extended to the server bind (plist + launch command)
    
    tests/CI:
    - #260 run a Postgres service container in CI so the durability/recovery tests
      exercise the real pooled PostgresSaver path and FAIL (not skip) when Postgres
      is unreachable; pin langgraph-checkpoint>=4,<5 + psycopg[binary,pool] in
      requirements-ci.txt so the resolver cannot drift

108	    # the reject→exploratory→gate_2 loop. failure_events has an append reducer, so
109	    # re-entering with the SAME unmet prereqs must NOT duplicate the failure event

151	    cp = MemorySaver()

1	"""
2	reasoning_client — the ADR-0016 LLM execution-routing seam.
3	
4	A single abstraction that isolates LLM auth/transport from agent logic. Agents
5	declare *what* to reason about and at *what stakes tier*; this seam decides
6	*where* the call runs, how it authenticates, how it retries, times out, and how
7	it is traced. No agent imports an LLM client directly — that coupling lives here
8	and only here (ADR-0016 §"Implementation seam").
9	
10	Tiers (routing is by stakes, per ADR-0016 / agents/MODEL_TIER_ASSIGNMENTS.md):
11	
12	    Tier.T1  — Deterministic ML. No LLM. complete() on T1 is a routing bug and
13	               raises: T1 work is computation, never inference.
14	    Tier.T2  — Local LLM on the Mac mini, 24/7 (Ollama). Bulk / low-stakes
15	               reasoning: per-pair classification, triage, drafting, review.
16	    Tier.T3  — Frontier Claude Max 20x via claude-agent-sdk. The `claude` CLI's
17	               subscription OAuth (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN) is the ONLY auth path
18	               — NO Anthropic API key. ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is scrubbed from the SDK
19	               subprocess env so a stray key can never spill to the metered API
20	               (ADR-0016). Reserved for high-stakes judgments. Bursty / queued.
21	
22	Reliability (hardening pass 1):
23	  - Every call has a per-tier timeout (#145, #158) so a hung backend can never
24	    block a synchronous LangGraph node forever.
25	  - Transient failures are retried with bounded exponential backoff (#157).
26	  - Every call is wrapped in an OpenTelemetry span carrying run_id/agent_id/tier
27	    (OpenInference LLM conventions) so BOTH tiers are visible in Phoenix — T3 is
28	    not LangChain-instrumented, so without this it would be invisible (#150, #168).
29	    Spans are cheap no-ops when no tracer provider is registered (offline/tests).
30	  - complete_json uses Ollama's native JSON mode on T2 and a balanced-brace
31	    extractor, instead of a greedy regex that mangled multi-value replies (#159).
32	
33	Data sovereignty (ADR-0002 as amended by ADR-0016): persistent artifacts never
34	leave the machine; T3 permits transient, non-retained inference over the specific
35	SCF-derived text needed for a single call.
36	
37	Public surface:
38	    complete(tier, prompt, system=..., run_id=..., agent_id=...) -> str
39	    complete_json(tier, prompt, ...) -> dict | list
40	    is_available(tier) -> bool
41	"""
42	
43	from __future__ import annotations
44	
45	import asyncio
46	import json
47	import os
48	import threading
49	import time
50	from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
51	from concurrent.futures import TimeoutError as FuturesTimeout
52	from enum import Enum
53	from functools import lru_cache
54	from typing import Any
55	
56	from opentelemetry import trace
57	
58	# ── Tiers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
59	
60	
61	class Tier(str, Enum):
62	    """Execution tier, by stakes. See agents/MODEL_TIER_ASSIGNMENTS.md."""
63	
64	    T1 = "t1"  # deterministic ML — no LLM
65	    T2 = "t2"  # local Ollama
66	    T3 = "t3"  # frontier Claude Max via Agent SDK
67	
68	
69	class ReasoningError(RuntimeError):
70	    """Raised when a reasoning call cannot be completed (backend down, timeout, T1 misuse)."""
71	
72	
73	class PermanentReasoningError(ReasoningError):
74	    """A non-transient failure (model missing, auth, CLI absent). Never retried."""
75	
76	
77	class RateLimitError(ReasoningError):
78	    """A Tier-3 subscription rate window. Retried with long backpressure, never
79	    spilled to a metered API (ADR-0016)."""
80	
81	
82	# ── Configuration (env-overridable; local-first defaults) ─────────────────────
83	
84	
85	def _env(name: str, default: str) -> str:
86	    val = os.environ.get(name, "").strip()
87	    return val or default
88	
89	
90	T2_MODEL = _env("HCGRC_T2_MODEL", "llama3.1:8b")
91	T2_BASE_URL = _env("HCGRC_OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434")
92	T3_MODEL = os.environ.get("HCGRC_T3_MODEL", "").strip() or None
93	
94	# Per-tier hard timeouts (seconds). T3 is bursty/queued so it gets more headroom.
95	# Clamped to a positive floor: a zero/negative override would raise ValueError
96	# ("Timeout value out of range") at future.result()/asyncio.wait_for() (pass-11),
97	# mirroring the backoff/retry clamps above.
98	T2_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("HCGRC_T2_TIMEOUT", "120")))
99	T3_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("HCGRC_T3_TIMEOUT", "300")))

100	
101	# Tier-2 (Ollama) decoding controls. num_ctx avoids silent truncation at the 2048
102	# default (#196); seed makes greedy decoding reproducible (#197); num_predict caps
103	# output so a runaway/JSON-mode generation hits a bound before the hard timeout (#198).
104	T2_NUM_CTX = int(_env("HCGRC_T2_NUM_CTX", "8192"))
105	T2_SEED = int(_env("HCGRC_T2_SEED", "0"))
106	T2_NUM_PREDICT = int(_env("HCGRC_T2_NUM_PREDICT", "2048"))
107	
108	# Bounded retry with exponential backoff on transient failures. Clamped >= 0 so a
109	# negative override cannot produce an empty loop that raises None (#208).
110	MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("HCGRC_REASONING_RETRIES", "2")))
111	# Clamp the backoff bases >= 0 too (like MAX_RETRIES): a negative override would feed
112	# a negative argument to time.sleep(), which raises ValueError mid-retry — uncaught,
113	# crashing the call (pass-8).
114	BACKOFF_BASE = max(0.0, float(_env("HCGRC_REASONING_BACKOFF", "1.5")))
115	# Tier-3 rate-window backpressure: long, capped waits — queue and resume, never
116	# fail fast onto a metered path.
117	RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF = max(0.0, float(_env("HCGRC_RATELIMIT_BACKOFF", "30")))
118	RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("HCGRC_RATELIMIT_RETRIES", "5")))
119	
120	# Truncation cap for prompt/response text recorded on spans (local Phoenix only).
121	_SPAN_TEXT_CAP = 8000
122	
123	_tracer = trace.get_tracer("hcgrc.reasoning_client")
124	
125	# Shared executor for enforcing sync-call timeouts. Not shut down: a hung backend
126	# call leaks one daemon worker rather than blocking the node (a ThreadPoolExecutor
127	# context-manager exit would join the hung thread, defeating the timeout).
128	_TIMEOUT_POOL_WORKERS = 8
129	_timeout_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(
130	    max_workers=_TIMEOUT_POOL_WORKERS, thread_name_prefix="reasoning-timeout"
131	)
132	
133	# In-flight worker accounting (#272). A timed-out call abandons (does NOT cancel)
134	# its worker — ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread blocked in
135	# llm.invoke/asyncio.run — so a truly-hung backend leaks that slot until (if ever)
136	# the call returns. Without a bound, accumulated leaks saturate the 8-slot pool and
137	# every subsequent submit silently queues behind work that never starts, converting
138	# ALL traffic into guaranteed timeouts. We track in-flight slots and fail FAST and
139	# LOUD with a distinct error when the pool is saturated, instead of degrading into a
140	# silent cliff. The per-tier inner timeouts (T2 HTTP timeout, T3 asyncio.wait_for)
141	# normally let workers self-terminate, so this guard only fires under genuine leaks.
142	_inflight_lock = threading.Lock()
143	_inflight = 0
144	
145	
146	def _submit_bounded(fn):
147	    """Submit to the shared pool, refusing if all worker slots are already in
148	    flight (i.e. leaked to hung backends). Decrements the count when the future
149	    completes — a leaked worker keeps its slot counted until it finally returns."""
150	    global _inflight
151	    with _inflight_lock:
152	        if _inflight >= _TIMEOUT_POOL_WORKERS:
153	            # Permanent, not transient: every worker is leaked to a hung backend, so
154	            # retrying with short backoff just wastes the retry budget before failing
155	            # anyway. Fail fast and loud (pass-7) — PermanentReasoningError breaks
156	            # complete()'s retry loop immediately.
157	            raise PermanentReasoningError(
158	                f"reasoning timeout pool saturated ({_inflight}/{_TIMEOUT_POOL_WORKERS} "
159	                "workers in-flight) — hung backend call(s) have leaked every worker slot"
160	            )
161	        _inflight += 1
162	
163	    def _release(_):
164	        global _inflight
165	        with _inflight_lock:
166	            _inflight -= 1
167	
168	    # Submit only after incrementing, but if submit() itself raises (e.g. the pool
169	    # was shut down — "cannot schedule new futures after shutdown") the done-callback
170	    # never fires, so decrement here to avoid permanently leaking the slot and
171	    # eventually false-saturating the pool (pass-5).
172	    try:
173	        future = _timeout_pool.submit(fn)
174	    except BaseException:
175	        with _inflight_lock:
176	            _inflight -= 1
177	        raise
178	    future.add_done_callback(_release)
179	    return future
180	
181	
182	# ── Timeout helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
183	
184	
185	def _with_timeout(fn, timeout: float, what: str):
186	    """Run a sync callable with a hard timeout; raise ReasoningError on overrun.
187	
188	    Propagates the current OpenTelemetry context into the worker thread so spans
189	    created by fn (e.g. the LangChain ChatOllama span) nest under the active
190	    reasoning.complete span instead of being orphaned (#238).
191	    """
192	    from opentelemetry import context as otel_context
193	
194	    ctx = otel_context.get_current()
195	
196	    def _run():
197	        token = otel_context.attach(ctx)
198	        try:
199	            return fn()
200	        finally:
201	            otel_context.detach(token)
202	
203	    future = _submit_bounded(_run)
204	    try:
205	        return future.result(timeout=timeout)
206	    except FuturesTimeout as exc:
207	        raise ReasoningError(f"{what} timed out after {timeout:.0f}s") from exc
208	
209	
210	def _run_sync(coro: Any) -> Any:
211	    """Run a coroutine to completion from sync code, even inside a running loop."""
212	    try:
213	        asyncio.get_running_loop()
214	    except RuntimeError:
215	        return asyncio.run(coro)
216	    # Bound the cross-thread wait so a starved/leaked pool can't deadlock a node
217	    # forever (#227). The inner coroutine already has its own asyncio timeout; this
218	    # is a backstop slightly beyond it. _submit_bounded fails fast if the pool is
219	    # already saturated with leaked workers rather than queueing forever (#272).
220	    return _submit_bounded(lambda: asyncio.run(coro)).result(timeout=T3_TIMEOUT + 30)
221	
222	
223	# ── Tier 2: local Ollama ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
224	
225	
226	@lru_cache(maxsize=16)
227	def _ollama(model: str, temperature: float, json_mode: bool) -> Any:
228	    """Construct (and cache) a ChatOllama client for the local serving stack."""
229	    try:
230	        from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama
231	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - dependency guard
232	        raise ReasoningError(
233	            "langchain-ollama is not installed — Tier 2 backend unavailable."
234	        ) from exc
235	    kwargs: dict[str, Any] = {
236	        "model": model,
237	        "temperature": temperature,
238	        "base_url": T2_BASE_URL,
239	        "num_ctx": T2_NUM_CTX,         # avoid silent truncation at the 2048 default (#196)
240	        "seed": T2_SEED,               # reproducible greedy decoding (#197)
241	        "client_kwargs": {"timeout": T2_TIMEOUT},  # native HTTP timeout (#195)
242	    }
243	    if json_mode:
244	        # Native structured-output: Ollama constrains decoding to valid JSON,
245	        # far more reliable than instructing + regex-extracting (#159, #175). Do
246	        # NOT cap num_predict here — format=json terminates at the end of a valid
247	        # JSON value, and a num_predict cap would truncate it into INVALID JSON
248	        # (#231). The hard timeout remains the runaway backstop.
249	        kwargs["format"] = "json"
250	    else:
251	        kwargs["num_predict"] = T2_NUM_PREDICT  # bound free-text output length (#198)
252	    return ChatOllama(**kwargs)
253	
254	
255	def _t2_complete(prompt: str, system: str | None, temperature: float, model: str,
256	                 json_mode: bool) -> tuple[str, dict[str, Any]]:
257	    # ADR-0002: the prompt is SCF-derived — refuse to send it to a non-loopback
258	    # Ollama endpoint (HCGRC_OLLAMA_BASE_URL could point off-box) (pass-7).
259	    from .infrastructure.config import require_local_uri
260	    require_local_uri(T2_BASE_URL, "Tier-2 Ollama (HCGRC_OLLAMA_BASE_URL)")
261	    llm = _ollama(model, temperature, json_mode)
262	    messages: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
263	    if system:
264	        messages.append(("system", system))
265	    messages.append(("human", prompt))
266	
267	    def _call():
268	        resp = llm.invoke(messages)
269	        content = getattr(resp, "content", resp)
270	        text = content if isinstance(content, str) else str(content)
271	        return text, getattr(resp, "usage_metadata", None)  # token counts (#279)
272	
273	    try:
274	        # Give the outer future timeout margin over the inner HTTP timeout
275	        # (client_kwargs timeout == T2_TIMEOUT) so the inner socket timeout wins and
276	        # the worker self-terminates cleanly, instead of racing exactly and risking
277	        # an abandoned (leaked) worker — mirrors the T3_TIMEOUT + 30 backstop (#272).
278	        text, usage = _with_timeout(
279	            _call, T2_TIMEOUT + 10, f"Tier 2 (Ollama @ {T2_BASE_URL})"
280	        )
281	        text = text.strip()
282	        if not text:
283	            # An empty/whitespace reply is not a valid result — surface it as a
284	            # retryable error rather than silently returning "" and corrupting a
285	            # JSON caller or recording an empty high-stakes answer (#233).
286	            raise ReasoningError(f"Tier 2 (Ollama @ {T2_BASE_URL}) returned an empty reply")
287	        return text, {"model": model, "usage": usage}
288	    except ReasoningError:
289	        raise
290	    except Exception as exc:
291	        msg = str(exc).lower()
292	        # A missing model is permanent — retrying just wastes backoff (#194).
293	        if "not found" in msg or "no such model" in msg or "try pulling" in msg:
294	            raise PermanentReasoningError(
295	                f"Tier 2 model {model!r} not available on Ollama: {exc}"
296	            ) from exc
297	        raise ReasoningError(f"Tier 2 (Ollama @ {T2_BASE_URL}) call failed: {exc}") from exc
298

1	"""
2	PostgresSaver configuration for HC-GRC.
3	
4	LangGraph uses the checkpointer to persist graph state between turns,
5	enabling interrupt/resume across the operator approval gates.
6	
7	Per ADR-0015 (#79): run_id propagates to PostgresSaver as thread_id.
8	Cross-store query procedure in RUNBOOK.md §5.
9	
10	LOCAL-FIRST CONSTRAINT: PostgreSQL instance runs on-device.
11	No state leaves the machine. SaaS checkpointers are disqualified.
12	
13	Connection config:
14	  Database: hcgrc
15	  Host: localhost (default)
16	  Port: 5432 (default)
17	
18	For Phase 0 testing: use MemorySaver (no Postgres required).
19	For Phase 1+: use PostgresSaver with the connection string below.
20	"""
21	
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	import atexit
25	import os
26	import re
27	import threading
28	
29	from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
30	
31	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes
32	# checkpoint schema migrations across concurrent initializers (get_checkpointer).
33	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4843_4752  # "HCGR"
34	
35	# Guards the process-local one-time-init bookkeeping below. The PG advisory lock
36	# serializes the DDL across PROCESSES, but the in-memory check-and-set guards
37	# (_atexit_registered, _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS) are not atomic on their own — two
38	# threads calling get_checkpointer() concurrently could both pass the check and
39	# double-register the atexit handler / double-run setup(). This lock makes those
40	# check-and-set sequences atomic within the process (pass-5).
41	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
42	
43	# Pools opened by get_checkpointer, closed once at process exit. A single atexit
44	# handler closes them all — registering one per call would accumulate handlers
45	# across repeated get_checkpointer() calls (#249).
46	_OPEN_POOLS: list = []
47	_atexit_registered = False
48	
49	# conn_strs whose schema bootstrap (advisory lock + setup() DDL + migration SELECT)
50	# has already run in this process. setup() is idempotent but not free — re-running
51	# it on every get_checkpointer() call takes a global advisory lock and replays the
52	# migration version query at each run start. Bootstrap once per process per schema;
53	# the advisory lock still guards the first-time cross-process migration race (#276).
54	_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS: set[str] = set()
55	
56	
57	# Strips `://user:password@` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. Allows '/'
58	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the
59	# userinfo terminator) and whitespace bound the match (pass-10 + its review).
60	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
61	
62	
63	def _redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
64	    """Remove credentials a backend exception (e.g. psycopg OperationalError) may
65	    embed in its message text. Two layers: an exact replacement of the known
66	    conn_str (precise, handles any password characters incl. spaces), then a generic
67	    URI-userinfo strip as a backstop for any other URI in the text."""
68	    if conn_str:
69	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
70	    return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
71	
72	
73	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
74	    """Return scheme://host:port/db with any user:password userinfo stripped, so a
75	    connection string carrying credentials is never surfaced in an error message —
76	    the conn_str is documented "never logged" (pass-10). IPv6 hosts are re-bracketed."""
77	    from urllib.parse import urlsplit
78	    try:
79	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
80	        host = u.hostname or ""
81	        if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
82	            host = f"[{host}]"
83	        netloc = f"{host}:{u.port}" if u.port else host
84	        return f"{u.scheme}://{netloc}{u.path}" if u.scheme else "<postgres>"
85	    except Exception:
86	        return "<postgres>"
87	
88	
89	def _safe_close(pool) -> None:
90	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing close() does not abort
91	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (pass-6). close() ultimately
92	    calls a C finish() that can raise."""
93	    try:
94	        pool.close()
95	    except Exception:
96	        pass
97	
98	
99	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool) -> None:
100	    global _atexit_registered

1	"""
2	Phoenix observability bootstrap for HC-GRC (roadmap Phase 0, deliverable #2).
3	
4	ADR-0009: Phoenix is the local, self-hosted LLM-tracing stack (LangSmith was
5	disqualified as SaaS-only). The Phoenix server runs on the compute node and
6	agent LLM calls export OpenTelemetry traces to it.
7	
8	Two responsibilities, deliberately separated:
9	
10	  1. The SERVER is a long-lived process — run it via the `phoenix serve` CLI
11	     (see launch_server_command()) or the launchd plist in scripts/. It is NOT
12	     launched from inside the application process.
13	
14	  2. INSTRUMENTATION is wired per-process via bootstrap_observability(), called
15	     once by each run entrypoint. It registers a global OTel tracer provider
16	     (BatchSpanProcessor — spans are buffered and exported in the background, so
17	     a momentary server outage drops spans rather than blocking or failing the
18	     run) pointed at the running server, and turns on the LangChain instrumentor.
19	     Because the provider is registered globally, the spans the reasoning_client
20	     emits for BOTH tiers — including Tier-3 (claude-agent-sdk), which LangChain
21	     instrumentation does not see — export to Phoenix too.
22	
23	run_id propagation (ADR-0015 #79): wrap a run in run_trace_context(run_id) so
24	every span produced during the run carries the run_id as session.id + metadata,
25	cross-referencing MLflow / PostgresSaver / PROV-DM.
26	"""
27	
28	from __future__ import annotations
29	
30	import atexit
31	import logging
32	import os
33	import signal
34	import sys
35	import threading
36	from contextlib import contextmanager
37	
38	# ADR-0002: traces carry SCF-derived content and must never leave the machine.
39	# The loopback guard is the single canonical validator in config.py, shared with
40	# Qdrant and MLflow (pass-5) so the data-sovereignty boundary is enforced
41	# identically everywhere; 0.0.0.0 is rejected (it binds all interfaces).
42	from ..config import LOOPBACK_HOSTS as _LOOPBACK_HOSTS  # noqa: F401  (kept for callers/tests)
43	from ..config import load_platform_config
44	from ..config import require_loopback as _require_loopback
45	
46	_log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
47	
48	# Cached registered provider — registration is idempotent within a process
49	# (repeat bootstrap calls return the same provider instead of double-registering
50	# the instrumentor, which would duplicate every span).
51	_PROVIDER = None
52	
53	
54	def _phoenix_cfg() -> dict:
55	    return load_platform_config()["observability"]["phoenix"]
56	
57	
58	def phoenix_base_url() -> str:
59	    """Return the Phoenix server base URL, e.g. http://localhost:6006."""
60	    cfg = _phoenix_cfg()
61	    return f"http://{cfg.get('host', 'localhost')}:{cfg.get('port', 6006)}"
62	
63	
64	def phoenix_endpoint() -> str:
65	    """Return the OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint the tracer provider exports to.
66	
67	    Validates loopback at the source (pass-5 #obs-wiring): this is public API and
68	    is called from the __main__ entrypoint and tests, not only from the already-
69	    guarded instrument_langchain(), so it must enforce its own ADR-0002 precondition."""
70	    cfg = _phoenix_cfg()
71	    _require_loopback(str(cfg.get("host", "localhost")), "Phoenix OTLP export")
72	    return f"{phoenix_base_url()}/v1/traces"
73	
74	
75	def launch_server_command() -> str:
76	    """Return the shell command that starts the persistent Phoenix server.
77	
78	    Validates the bind host is loopback (#283): the server bind, not just the
79	    client export host, is what determines whether traces are reachable off-box.
80	    """
81	    cfg = _phoenix_cfg()
82	    host = _require_loopback(str(cfg.get("host", "localhost")), "server bind")
83	    return f"phoenix serve --host {host} --port {cfg.get('port', 6006)}"
84	
85	
86	def instrument_langchain(project_name: str | None = None):
87	    """
88	    Register a global OTel tracer provider pointed at the Phoenix server (using a
89	    background BatchSpanProcessor) and enable LangChain tracing for this process.
90	
91	    Idempotent: the first call registers; subsequent calls return the cached
92	    provider without re-instrumenting (so spans are never duplicated).
93	
94	    Returns the tracer provider.
95	    """
96	    global _PROVIDER
97	    if _PROVIDER is not None:
98	        return _PROVIDER
99	
100	    from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor
101	    from opentelemetry import trace as _trace
102	    from phoenix.otel import register
103	
104	    cfg = _phoenix_cfg()
105	    # ADR-0002 data sovereignty: refuse to export SCF-derived traces off-box.
106	    _require_loopback(str(cfg.get("host", "localhost")), "OTLP export")
107	
108	    # Bound the OTLP/HTTP exporter so a downed Phoenix server cannot block process
109	    # teardown for the exporter's default 10s on the shutdown flush. The installed
110	    # OTel BatchProcessor.force_flush() ignores its timeout_millis argument, so the
111	    # only effective cap is at the exporter level (#277). Don't clobber an explicit
112	    # operator override.
113	    os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
114	
115	    provider = register(
116	        project_name=project_name or cfg.get("project_name", "hc-grc"),
117	        endpoint=phoenix_endpoint(),
118	        batch=True,                        # BatchSpanProcessor: buffer + async export
119	        set_global_tracer_provider=True,   # so reasoning_client spans export too
120	    )
121	    # set_tracer_provider can only succeed once per process; if a competing global
122	    # provider already won the race, register() still RETURNS the Phoenix provider
123	    # (so LangChain spans, passed it explicitly, still reach Phoenix) but the GLOBAL
124	    # provider is something else — and reasoning_client's module-level tracer resolves
125	    # through the global, so Tier-3 frontier spans (their ONLY record) would silently
126	    # vanish. Surface that collision instead of dropping them silently (#278).
127	    if _trace.get_tracer_provider() is not provider:
128	        _log.warning(
129	            "Phoenix did not become the global OTel tracer provider — a competing "
130	            "provider was registered first. Tier-3 reasoning_client spans will not "
131	            "reach Phoenix. Ensure bootstrap_observability() runs before any other "
132	            "OTel instrumentation."
133	        )
134	    LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=provider, skip_dep_check=True)
135	    # Flush buffered spans at process exit so the BatchSpanProcessor does not drop
136	    # the tail of a run on a clean exit / SIGINT (#215). atexit does NOT run on
137	    # SIGTERM (launchd/kill/container-stop), so also install a SIGTERM handler that
138	    # flushes then exits — otherwise the tail of a killed run is silently lost
139	    # (#281). Both flushes are bounded by the exporter timeout set above (#277).
140	    if hasattr(provider, "force_flush"):
141	        atexit.register(provider.force_flush)
142	        _install_sigterm_flush(provider)
143	    _PROVIDER = provider
144	    return provider
145	
146	
147	def _install_sigterm_flush(provider) -> None:
148	    """Flush + shut down the span processor on SIGTERM before the process exits.
149	
150	    Signal handlers can only be installed from the main thread; if we're not on it
151	    (e.g. instrumentation wired from a worker), skip silently — atexit still covers
152	    the clean-exit and SIGINT paths."""
153	    if threading.current_thread() is not threading.main_thread():
154	        return
155	
156	    def _handler(_signum, _frame):
157	        try:
158	            provider.force_flush()
159	            if hasattr(provider, "shutdown"):
160	                provider.shutdown()
161	        finally:
162	            sys.exit(0)
163	
164	    try:
165	        signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
166	    except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - non-main-thread / unsupported
167	        pass
168	
169	
170	def bootstrap_observability():
171	    """
172	    Idempotently wire tracing for the current process. Called once by each run
173	    entrypoint (graph runners). Degrades to a no-op — never raising — when tracing
174	    is disabled by config, switched off via HCGRC_DISABLE_TRACING, or the
175	    instrumentation stack cannot be initialized. Returns the provider or None.
176	    """
177	    if os.environ.get("HCGRC_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip():
178	        return None
179	    try:
180	        cfg = _phoenix_cfg()
181	    except Exception:
182	        return None
183	    if not cfg.get("enabled", True):
184	        return None
185	    # Also point MLflow at the local store so experiment tracking is actually
186	    # configured in a run path (cross-store run_id correlation, #282). Best-effort.
187	    try:
188	        from ..tracking.mlflow_setup import configure_mlflow
189	        configure_mlflow()
190	    except Exception:
191	        pass
192	    try:
193	        return instrument_langchain()
194	    except Exception:
195	        # Missing deps, server unreachable at registration, etc. — tracing is
196	        # best-effort observability and must never block the research run.
197	        return None
198	
199	
200	@contextmanager
201	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None):
202	    """
203	    Context manager that stamps run_id onto every span produced within it, for
204	    cross-store correlation. Sets it two ways so BOTH span sources pick it up:
205	    OpenInference using_attributes (LangChain auto-spans → session.id) AND OTel
206	    baggage `hcgrc.run_id`, which the reasoning_client seam reads as a fallback so
207	    its manual T2/T3 spans carry the run_id even when the caller didn't pass it
208	    explicitly (#239). No-op if run_id is None or the helpers are unavailable.
209	    """
210	    if not run_id:
211	        yield
212	        return
213	    from contextlib import ExitStack
214	
215	    from opentelemetry import baggage, context
216	
217	    with ExitStack() as stack:
218	        try:
219	            from openinference.instrumentation import using_attributes
220	            stack.enter_context(using_attributes(session_id=run_id, metadata={"run_id": run_id}))
221	        except Exception:
222	            pass
223	        token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("hcgrc.run_id", run_id))
224	        try:
225	            yield
226	        finally:
227	            context.detach(token)
228	
229	
230	if __name__ == "__main__":  # pragma: no cover - operational entrypoint
231	    print(f"Phoenix base URL:    {phoenix_base_url()}")
232	    print(f"OTLP traces endpoint: {phoenix_endpoint()}")
233	    print(f"Start the server with: {launch_server_command()}")

1	"""
2	Gate nodes for the HC-GRC research platform.
3	
4	Five gates enforce human approval at structural transition points.
5	Each gate issues a LangGraph interrupt(), surfacing a structured proposal
6	to the operator. The operator responds via Claude on any device (ADR-0014).
7	
8	Gate decisions are written by gate_coordinator_node — never directly here.
9	On rejection, each gate appends a failure_event for Agent Evolution monitoring.
10	
11	Gate definitions:
12	  Gate 1 — Pre-data checkpoint (Phase 0 complete, all prerequisites verified)
13	  Gate 2 — Exploratory → Confirmatory (hypothesis lock, test data unlock)
14	  Gate 3 — Finding significance review (before publication pipeline)
15	  Gate 4 — Manuscript submission approval
16	  Gate 5 — Escalation resolution gate (triggered by ADR-0014 Escalation loop)
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime
22	from typing import Any
23	
24	from langgraph.types import interrupt
25	
26	from ..state import HCGRCState
27	
28	# ── Shared helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	
31	def _build_gate_proposal(gate_id: str, title: str, summary: str,
32	                          checklist: list[str], run_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
33	    """Build the structured proposal surfaced to the operator via interrupt()."""
34	    return {
35	        "gate_id": gate_id,
36	        "title": title,
37	        "summary": summary,
38	        "checklist": checklist,
39	        "run_id": run_id,
40	        "timestamp_utc": datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
41	        "instructions": (
42	            "Respond with: {'decision': 'approved'|'rejected'|'deferred', "
43	            "'rationale': '<your reasoning>'}"
44	        ),
45	    }
46	
47	
48	def _rejection_event(gate_id: str, rationale: str, run_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
49	    """Failure event appended to state for Agent Evolution monitoring."""
50	    return {
51	        "event_type": "gate_rejection",
52	        "gate_id": gate_id,
53	        "rationale": rationale,
54	        "run_id": run_id,
55	        "timestamp_utc": datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
56	    }
57	
58	
59	_VALID_DECISIONS = {"approved", "rejected", "deferred"}
60	# Only 'approved' is terminal — it stops the gate re-firing on re-entry (don't
61	# re-unlock). 'rejected' routes back for re-review and 'deferred' parks the run for
62	# the operator to re-trigger later; BOTH must re-fire the interrupt when the gate
63	# is re-entered, so neither is terminal (pass-3 #5).
64	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = {"approved"}
65	
66	
67	def _parse_operator_response(resp: Any, gate_id: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
68	    """
69	    Validate the operator's interrupt() response and return (decision, rationale).
70	
71	    A malformed response must NOT be silently coerced to 'rejected' (that would
72	    let a non-dict or a typo'd decision masquerade as a real governance decision,
73	    #163/#180). Fail loud so the operator re-submits a well-formed response on the
74	    next resume.
75	    """
76	    if not isinstance(resp, dict):
77	        raise ValueError(
78	            f"{gate_id}: operator response must be a dict like "
79	            f"{{'decision': 'approved'|'rejected'|'deferred', 'rationale': '...'}}, "
80	            f"got {type(resp).__name__}."
81	        )
82	    decision = resp.get("decision")
83	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
84	        raise ValueError(
85	            f"{gate_id}: operator response 'decision' must be one of "
86	            f"{sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)}, got {decision!r}."
87	        )
88	    rationale = resp.get("rationale") or "No rationale provided."
89	    return decision, rationale
90	
91	
92	def _already_decided(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str) -> bool:
93	    """
94	    True if this gate already holds a TERMINAL decision (approved only).
95	
96	    Guards against a re-invocation re-firing the interrupt and re-running
97	    downstream effects on top of a terminal decision (#177). A 'rejected' record
98	    is deliberately NOT terminal: the Gate-2 reject → revise → re-review loop
99	    (route_after_gate_2 → exploratory → … → gate_2) must re-fire the interrupt so
100	    the operator is re-prompted, otherwise the gate short-circuits to {} and the
101	    graph spins until GraphRecursionError (pass-2 #184/#187). The loop-back path
102	    is responsible for producing the revised state that warrants re-review.
103	    """
104	    record = state.get("gate_status", {}).get(gate_id)
105	    return bool(record) and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS
106	
107	
108	def _finalize_gate(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str, decision: str,
109	                   rationale: str, reviewer: str = "operator") -> dict[str, Any]:
110	    """Build the gate node's state update: failure event (if not approved) + the
111	    authoritative gate_status record (written only by gate_coordinator_node)."""
112	    update: dict[str, Any] = {}
113	    if decision == "rejected":
114	        # 'deferred' is a park, not a failure — only a rejection is a failure_event
115	        # (a deferred decision recorded as a rejection mislabels the audit trail).
116	        update["failure_events"] = [_rejection_event(gate_id, rationale, state["run_id"])]
117	    from .gate_coordinator import gate_coordinator_node
118	    update.update(gate_coordinator_node(state, gate_id, decision, rationale, reviewer=reviewer))
119	    return update
120	
121	
122	def _run_gate(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str, proposal: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
123	    """
124	    Drive one gate: idempotency guard, then interrupt() the operator and finalize.
125	
126	    Validation runs INSIDE a re-prompt loop (pass-3 #3): interrupt() consumes and
127	    persists the resume value before validation, so raising on a malformed value
128	    would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever. Instead, a malformed
129	    or mis-correlated response re-fires a fresh interrupt (next resume index)
130	    carrying the error, so the operator can re-submit and actually recover.
131	
132	    Gate-id correlation (pass-3 #4): if the operator response names a gate, it must
133	    match this gate — a stale/misrouted decision cannot be applied to the wrong gate.
134	    """
135	    if _already_decided(state, gate_id):
136	        return {}
137	    current = proposal
138	    while True:
139	        resp = interrupt(current)
140	        if isinstance(resp, dict) and resp.get("gate_id") not in (None, gate_id):
141	            current = {**proposal, "error": (
142	                f"response gate_id {resp.get('gate_id')!r} does not match {gate_id}")}
143	            continue
144	        try:
145	            decision, rationale = _parse_operator_response(resp, gate_id)
146	        except ValueError as exc:
147	            current = {**proposal, "error": str(exc)}
148	            continue
149	        return _finalize_gate(state, gate_id, decision, rationale)

Now I have enough information. Let me compile my findings into the structured output:

findings.title: SIGTERM handler body untested (missing invocation)
findings.problem: The _handler function registered by _install_sigterm_flush() is never invoked in tests. This critical failure path must call provider.force_flush(), conditionally provider.shutdown(), and sys.exit(0), but test_install_sigterm_flush_registers_on_main_thread only verifies the handler is registered and callable, not that the body executes correctly or handles exceptions.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of failure/recovery branches: the SIGTERM flush path is essential for graceful shutdown under SIGTERM (launchd/kill/container-stop) but is untested.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_sigterm_handler_calls_force_flush_and_shutdown() that invokes the captured handler and verifies: (1) provider.force_flush() is called, (2) provider.shutdown() is called if it exists, (3) sys.exit(0) is invoked. Use monkeypatch to capture sys.exit and verify its call.

findings.title: ChatOllama socket timeout exception handling not tested
findings.problem: When ChatOllama.invoke() raises a socket timeout (socket.timeout -> TimeoutError), the code catches it in the except Exception handler (line 290) and converts to ReasoningError for retry. However, there is no test that injects a socket timeout to verify this path works. The critical timeout margin (T2_TIMEOUT + 10 outer vs. T2_TIMEOUT inner HTTP timeout) is designed so the HTTP timeout fires first, but the recovery behavior is untested.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of failure/recovery branches: socket timeouts are a common failure mode for HTTP clients but have no coverage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_socket_timeout_triggers_retry() that mocks ChatOllama.invoke() to raise socket.timeout, and verify it is caught, wrapped as ReasoningError, and retried (not treated as permanent).

findings.title: ChatOllama json_mode branch not exercised in tests
findings.problem: The _ollama() function's json_mode branch (kwargs['format'] = 'json') is used by complete_json() calls, but all tests mock _attempt() instead of exercising the real path. The json_mode=True case creates ChatOllama with format=json, which is never instantiated in tests.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage: the native JSON mode for Ollama is a critical feature (#159, #175) but has zero path coverage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_json_mode_configures_format(monkeypatch) that doesn't mock _attempt, instead patches ChatOllama.__init__ to capture kwargs and verify format='json' is set when json_mode=True.

findings.title: T2_TIMEOUT + 10 margin invariant not validated
findings.problem: The code comment states 'Give the outer future timeout margin over the inner HTTP timeout so the inner socket timeout wins' but there is no test validating this critical invariant. The margin exists (T2_TIMEOUT + 10 vs. T2_TIMEOUT), but the behavior when the inner timeout fires first is not tested (see socket timeout issue above).
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of timeout ordering: the timeout margin is a correctness property (prevents worker abandonment) but has no validation test.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_timeout_margin_ensures_inner_fires_first(monkeypatch) that verifies T2_TIMEOUT < T2_TIMEOUT + 10 and documents the intended timeout ordering. Combined with the socket timeout test above, this validates the margin is effective.

findings.title: Pool slot release on exception not fully covered
findings.problem: test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_on_completion uses a polling loop with real time.sleep(0.01) waits, which can be racy if the done callback is delayed. While the 2-second deadline makes it unlikely to fail in practice, the callback mechanics under exception propagation are not explicitly tested.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test reliability: polling with real sleeps can introduce non-determinism.
findings.proposed_fix: Use a threading.Event instead of polling to synchronously verify the done callback fires when the future completes. Example: set an Event in the callback, then assert the event is set after future.result().
